coredata.dc:title: Atomic collapse in graphene quantum dots in a magnetic field
coredata.dc:creator: Eren, I.
Güçlü, A.D.
coredata.dc:description:
We investigate finite size and external magnetic field effects on the atomic collapse due to a Coulomb impurity placed at the center of a hexagonal graphene quantum dot within tight binding and mean-field Hubbard approaches. For large quantum dots, the atomic collapse effect persists when the magnetic field is present, characterized by a series of Landau level crossings and anticrossings, in agreement with previous bulk graphene results. However, we show that a new regime arises if the size of the quantum dot is comparable to or smaller than the magnetic length: While the lowest bound states cross the Fermi level at a lower value of coupling constant
β < 0 . 5
, a size independent critical coupling constant
β
c
∗
> 0 . 5
emerges in the local density of states spectrum, which increases with the applied magnetic field. These effects are found to be persistent in the presence of electron–electron interactions within mean-field Hubbard approximation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Atomic collapse
Magnetic field
Nanostructures
Electronic structure

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Emergence of the resonant states in zero magnetic field

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
(a) Representation of a GQD with a Coulomb potential at center, in external magnetic field. (b–c) Energy spectra as a function of coupling constant without the magnetic field, for a GQD with radius (b) 2.28 nm, (c) 3.98 nm, and (d) 7.38 nm. (e–g) Corresponding LDOS at the neighboring atoms of the impurity center. Dashed red lines represents the location of the
β
c
= 1 / 2
where the lowest bound state crosses the Fermi level. Solid red line in (g) represents the position of the resonance state.

LDOS near the impurity center for GQD with 7.38 nm radius for magnetic field values (a) B = 0 T, (b) B = 10 T
(
l
B
= 8.22 nm) and (c) B = 250 T
(
l
B
= 1.64 nm).
Evolution of LBS towards resonant channel as a function of coupling constant for different magnetic field values obtained using (a) TB method and (b) MFH method.
Critical coupling constant for the LBS of different size of GQDs as a function of magnetic field obtained using TB (solid blue line) and MFH results (dashed red line).
Magnetic field value expected for the formation of LL for various sizes of GQDs studied in this work.

head.ce:title: Atomic collapse in graphene quantum dots in a magnetic field

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Izmir Institute of Technology, Izmir, 35430, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Izmir Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Izmir Institute of Technology Department of Physics

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
We investigate finite size and external magnetic field effects on the atomic collapse due to a Coulomb impurity placed at the center of a hexagonal graphene quantum dot within tight binding and mean-field Hubbard approaches. For large quantum dots, the atomic collapse effect persists when the magnetic field is present, characterized by a series of Landau level crossings and anticrossings, in agreement with previous bulk graphene results. However, we show that a new regime arises if the size of the quantum dot is comparable to or smaller than the magnetic length: While the lowest bound states cross the Fermi level at a lower value of coupling constant
β < 0 . 5
, a size independent critical coupling constant
β
c
∗
> 0 . 5
emerges in the local density of states spectrum, which increases with the applied magnetic field. These effects are found to be persistent in the presence of electron–electron interactions within mean-field Hubbard approximation.

ce:section.ce:para:
Atomic collapse is a fundamental quantum relativistic phenomenon expected to occur in ultra-heavy nuclei above a critical charge
Z
c
= 1 / α ∼ 172
where
α =
e
2
/ ħ c
is the fine-structure constant [1–4]. Above the critical charge, it is predicted that the formerly lowest bound state
1
S
1 / 2
dives into the negative energy Dirac sea, becoming a resonant state. However, since the heavy atom requirement is a major challenge from the experimental perspective, direct observation of these collapse states has so far remained elusive in high-energy heavy-ion collision experiments [5,6]. On the other hand, electrons near K and K’ points of graphene have a relativistic speed [7,8], almost 300 slower than the speed of light in a vacuum, exhibiting quantum relativistic phenomena such as Klein paradox [9], Zitterbewegung [10] and in particular, atomic collapse [11–17]. In fact, graphene forms a
2 + 1
dimensional system for which the critical coupling constant
β
c
=
Z
c
α
g
becomes
1 / 2
with a larger fine-structure constant
α
g
= 2 . 2 / κ
, where κ is the dielectric constant [11,12]. Thus, unlike in quantum electrodynamics,
Z
c
is expected to be of the order of unity in graphene, making it an ideal condensed matter platform to investigate the atomic collapse in the form of quasi-bound states. Such quasi-bound states were observed around clusters of charged calcium (Ca) dimers on graphene via the local density of states (LDOS) [14] as well as around positively charged carbon vacancies by applying voltage pulses of 2 to 3 V for
> 10
s with the help of a scanning tunneling microscope tip [15].

Another interesting aspect of atomic collapse is the effect of an external magnetic field. While the magnetic field is expected to reduce the critical charge
Z
c
in
3 + 1
dimension [18], the situation is different in graphene due to the formation of Landau levels in
2 + 1
dimension [19,20]. While some previous results based on perturbation approach predicts that the value of the critical charge should vanish for massless particles under any value of magnetic field [21], others argued that the critical charge will remain unchanged [22–24]. Recent theoretical calculations by Moldovan et al. [19] based on tight-binding method which allows the treatment of subcritical (
Z <
Z
c
) and supercritical (
Z >
Z
c
) regimes on equal footing confirm the prediction that
Z
c
should not change.

In this work, we address the question of finite size dependence of the atomic collapse state in graphene in a magnetic field which is, as far as we know, not yet discussed in the literature. Without the magnetic field, the problem of a graphene quantum dot with a Coulomb impurity (see Fig. 1(a)) was previously investigated theoretically within effective mass approximation [25], and later using tight-binding and mean-field Hubbard approximations [26–28]. Although the energy spectrum of finite sized graphene is discrete with a non-zero gap at the Dirac point, atomic collapse states can still be observed and characterized through the evolution of lowest bound states (LBS) into the hole states [25–28]. Strikingly, it was found that the critical coupling constant remains size independent in all three approaches, giving
β
c
= 0 . 5
without electron interaction effects [25] and
β
c
∼ 0 . 6
when long-range electron–electron interactions are included [26]. On the other hand, in the presence of a magnetic field, the magnetic length scale
l
B
= 26 nm /
B
, where B is given in Tesla, is expected to affect the confinement of electrons by the finite size of the graphene quantum dot (
R
G Q D
) which may lead to new effects. Here, we use tight-binding (TB) and mean-field Hubbard (MFH) approaches to investigate the combined effect of magnetic field and system size of atomic collapse in hexagonal graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with armchair edges. Such type of GQDs are free of localized edge states, serving as a bridge between bulk graphene and finite-size samples [29,30]. By observing the evolution of the lowest bound state (LBS) to a resonant state, we find that (i) the local density of states (LDOS) spectra exhibit two distinct regimes,
l
B
>
R
G Q D
and
l
B
<
R
G Q D
, and more interestingly, a new critical coupling constant
β
c
emerges that increases with the magnetic field for
l
B
>
R
G Q D
.

The rest of this paper is organized as follows. In Section 2 computational models based on TB and MFH approaches are introduced. The effects of magnetic field in relation to the size of GQDs and the effects of electron–electron interactions are discussed in Section 3. Finally, Section 4 consists of our conclusions.

We describe graphene quantum dots using the nearest neighbor tight-binding model where the effect of a perpendicular magnetic field is incorporated by using Peierls substitution and the effect of electron–electron interactions are treated within mean-field Hubbard model:
(1)
H =
∑
〈 i , j 〉
σ
τ
ˆ
i j
e
i 2 π
φ
i j
c
i σ
†
c
j σ
− ħ
v
F
β
∑
i , σ
c
i σ
†
c
i σ
r
i
+ U
∑
i , σ
(
〈
n
i
σ
̄
〉
−
1
2
)
n
i σ
where the hopping parameter
τ
ˆ
i j
= t
e
i 2 π
φ
i j
includes the Peierls phase
φ
i j
=
e
h c
∫
r
i
r
j
A d l
with
A =
( − y , x , 0 )
B
z
/ 2
[7,19,31], responsible of magnetic confinement and Landau level formation. Without the magnetic field, the nearest neighbor hopping parameter is taken to be
t = − 2 . 8
eV
.
c
i σ
†
is the creation operator of an electron at lattice site i with spin σ . Finite size and geometrical effects are included through the specific choice of lattice sites in the summation over i . The onsite Hubbard parameter due to two electrons occupying the same lattice site is taken to be
U = 2 . 75
eV
, and
n
i σ
is the number operator. The last term represents the Coulomb potential generated by the central impurity with charge Z (see Fig. 1(a)) expressed in terms of dimensionless coupling constant β and Fermi velocity
v
F
, for convenience. In our calculations, we use β as a control parameter which can be tuned experimentally by changing the charge Z , or by controlling the relative permittivity through a gate voltage [19,20]. Numerical calculations are performed for hexagonal armchair GQDs of different sizes up to 10806 atoms for TB calculations and up to 5514 atoms for self-consistent Hubbard calculations to find the quasi-particle states
ψ
n
( r )
with energies
E
n
. The size of GQDs are characterized by radius
R
G Q D
and will be compared to the magnetic length
l
B
. Different sizes of GQDs studied in this work and corresponding minimum magnetic field necessary for Landau level formation is given in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emergence of the resonant states in zero magnetic field
ce:section.ce:para:
Before investigating the effect of magnetic field, we briefly review the evolution of the LBS towards the resonant state in the absence of magnetic field within TB model. Experimentally, the transition from subcritical to supercritical regime can be observed as a formation of a resonant peak around the impurity charge in LDOS, accessible through a scanning tunneling microscope. We calculate the LDOS using the formula
N
( E , r )
=
∑
n
|
ψ
n
( r )
|
2
δ
( E −
E
n
)
, averaged over six closest lattice site around the charge impurity. For finite sized systems studied here, the energy spectrum is discrete (Figs. 1(b-d) for
R = 2 . 28
nm
, 3.98 nm and 7.38 nm, respectively) and a gap is present at the Dirac point unlike the relativistic picture. Nevertheless, the formation of a resonant channel can still be observed as the LBS dives into and hybridizes with the holes states as seen in Figs. 1(e-g). As the system size increases, the density of the discrete states increases and the resonant channel becomes more prominent especially in the supercritical regime (
β
c
> 0 . 5
), as shown with the solid red line in Fig. 1(g). The critical coupling value
β
c
= 0 . 5
where the LBS crosses the Dirac point, is size independent in agreement with previous calculations [25,26]. We note that, although the resonant state has contributions from several quasi-bound states diving into and hybridizing with the hole states, the main contribution is due to the LBS near
β
c
.

We now focus on the effect of an external magnetic field on the evolution of the LBS towards resonant state. Figs. 2(a–c) show the LDOS of a GQD with
7 . 38
nm
(
N
A
= 5514
atoms, see Table 1) radius with magnetic field
B = 0
T,
B = 10
T, and
B = 250
T, respectively. While the LDOS for
B = 10
T is not much different from the LDOS for
B = 0
T (finer differences will be discussed below), the
B = 250
T results are clearly different. Indeed, for
B = 250
T, the LDOS is strikingly similar to the bulk LDOS results at lower magnetic fields [19]: At
β = 0
, there is formation of Landau levels N . As β is increased, the Landau level
N = 1
splits into orbital states
m = − 1
and
m = 0
, followed by the splitting of higher N states. The system then enters the supercritical regime, accompanied by a series of Landau level crossings and anticrossings.

In order to understand the behavior of LDOS at lower magnetic fields for a given GQD size
R
G Q D
, it is useful to compare the magnetic field length
l
B
to
R
G Q D
. For
B = 10
T, we find
l
B
= 8 . 22
nm
, which is close to but lower than
R
G Q D
= 7 . 38
nm
. Thus, size quantization dominates the spectrum, preventing the formation of Landau levels. For a more detailed understanding of the interplay between the magnetic field and GQD size, in Fig. 3 we investigate the transition from subcritical to supercritical regime more systematically. The curves represent the evolution of LBS (or lowest Landau level in case of high magnetic field) towards resonant channel obtained from LDOS for different
R
G Q D
and B , obtained from TB (upper panel) and mean-field Hubbard (lower panel) calculations. At
B = 0
, as discussed before, the LBS for all GQD sizes crosses the Fermi level at the critical value
β
c
∼ 0 . 5
(
β
c
∼ 0 . 55
for the smallest dot with
N
A
= 42
atoms). However, when the magnetic field is turned on, two effects are observed: (i) For
l
B
<
R
G Q D
(red dotted curves), the LBS is now the
N = 0
Landau level with energy close to zero for small β values, and thus behaves differently than the LBS with
l
B
>
R
G Q D
; (ii) the LBS’s with
R
G Q D
<
l
B
(blue solid lines) do not cross the Fermi level at the same coupling constant
β
c
= 0 . 5
, but there is a new critical value that we will denote by
β
c
∗
> 0 . 5
where LBS’s cross each other below the Fermi level. This latter can be explained by the fact that although the magnetic field is not strong enough to give rise to Landau levels for
R
G Q D
<
l
B
, the shrinking of the orbitals still occurs, mainly affecting the larger size dots and pulling down their energies stronger. As a result, the value of
β
c
∗
decreases and the crossing occurs at lower energies as B increases.

The same effects explained above are also seen in mean-field Hubbard calculations, shown in Fig. 3(b). The quantitative difference between the TB and mean-field Hubbard results is also minimal showing that the effects described are stable against onsite electron–electron interactions. Fig. 4 shows the finer details of the evolution of
β
c
∗
as a function of B obtained by TB and Hubbard results. As B is increased from zero to 250 T,
β
c
increases from 0.5 to
∼ 0 . 65
with a slightly larger value when Hubbard interactions are present. We note, however that, in mean-field Hubbard calculations presented here, the long range electron–electron interactions are not included. Long range interactions are found to increase
β
c
by
∼ 0 . 1
in the absence of magnetic field. We expect a similar increase in
β
c
∗
to occur in the presence of long range interactions.

In summary, we employed TB and MFH methods to investigate the combined effect of finite size and magnetic field on the atomic collapse in GQDs in a magnetic field as the coupling constant strength is increased. We demonstrated the emergence of two distinct regimes in LDOS data, corresponding to
l
B
<
R
G Q D
and
l
B
>
R
G Q D
. If
l
B
<
R
G Q D
, Landau level formation is possible and LDOS exhibits similar behavior as the atomic collapse in bulk graphene. If
l
B
>
R
G Q D
, however, there is a new critical coupling strength value
β
c
∗
> 0 . 5
which acts as a crossing point below Fermi level for different GQD sizes and increases with B . These effects are found to be persistent in the presence of electron–electron interactions and should be detectable in scanning tunneling microscope experiments. Our results may help to understand the quantum behavior of charge impurities in external magnetic fields which can also be important for the design of future nanoelectronic devices based on graphene.
I. Eren: Writing, Original draft, Software, Visualization, Investigation. A.D. Güçlü: Supervision, Methodology, Reviewing and editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by The Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK) under the 1001 Grant Project No. 119F119.
